---
title: "Module 2 - Team Formation and Project Onboarding"
draft: false
class: "402"

---

# Module Orientation

In this module, you will meet your team, decide how to work together as a team, and choose the health problem that your team wants to focus on this term. 
# What You Should Be Able to Do After This Module

By the end of this module, you should be able to:

- Describe what makes an effective team.
- Know what health problem your team has chosen.
- Understand and be comfortable with the requirements of your team agreement.
# Core Concepts and Materials

## Developing a Team Agreement

This course is designed as a problem-based learning (PBL) course. PBL is an approach that teaches a topic by trying to solve real-world problems.  You are going to learn the basics of the US health care system by trying to solve a real-world health problem.  PBL relies on you working effectively on a team together.

Problem-based learning requires teams to think together, not simply divide tasks and stitch them together later. Strong teams create explicit agreements about how they will work, communicate, make decisions, and hold each other accountable. This page outlines the practices that consistently lead to successful teams—and the pitfalls that undermine them.

Use this page as a reference as you develop your team's working agreement.
### Why Team Agreements Matter

A team agreement is a shared document that makes expectations explicit. It helps prevent misunderstandings, diffuses tension before it builds, and gives the team an anchor when challenges arise. Think of it as the operating manual your group writes for itself.
### Core Elements of Effective Teamwork

**1. Shared Understanding Comes First**  
Before dividing tasks, the team must discuss the problem together. Everyone should understand the goals, the rationale behind your approach, and the key decisions. A strong project always begins with a shared analysis.

**2. Communication Must Be Reliable and Transparent**  
Good teams don’t rely on luck or last-minute scrambling. Establish norms such as:  
• What communication platform you will use  
• Expected response times  
• How you will document decisions and who will do it  
• Frequency and format of check-ins  
Consistent communication prevents most problems.

**3. Collaboration Beats “Divide and Assemble”**  
Teams that simply split up the project produce fragmented, inconsistent work. Instead:  
• Plan the structure and argument together  
• Review each member’s work as a group  
• Revise collaboratively so the final product speaks with one voice  
This approach mirrors real-world team-based problem solving.

**4. Accountability Is Shared and Explicit**  
Accountability is not punishment—it is clarity. A strong team agreement includes:  
• Individual responsibilities and deadlines  
• What happens if someone falls behind  
• How the team will redistribute tasks when needed  
• How concerns will be raised and addressed  
Teams succeed when expectations are visible and mutual.
### Building a Strong Team Agreement: Key Tips

**Set Clear Roles, But Stay Flexible**  
Assign roles such as coordinator, writer/editor, researcher, or data lead, but revisit them as the project evolves. Roles are tools, not cages.

**Name Your Internal Deadlines**  
Set earlier deadlines than the official ones. Give yourselves time to review, synthesize, and revise.

**Decide How You Will Handle Conflict**  
Conflict is normal. Avoiding it is harmful. Agree on how you will raise concerns, how disagreements will be resolved, and when to involve the instructor.

**Define Quality Standards Together**  
Clarify what “good work” means for your team. Alignment reduces frustration and mismatched expectations.

**Plan for the Unexpected**  
People get sick, overloaded, or stuck. A good agreement includes what the team will do—not who to blame—when things change.
### Common Pitfalls to Avoid

Teams typically struggle when they:  

• Split the project into isolated parts without shared discussion  
• Rely on one or two members to carry the work  
• Assume “someone else” is handling a task  
• Fail to communicate early when problems arise  
• Wait until the last minute to coordinate

These issues are preventable when the team agreement is clear, specific, and used actively—not just created and forgotten.
### Using the Agreement Throughout the Project

A team agreement is a living document. Revisit it regularly:

• At the start of each major project phase  
• When challenges emerge  
• When team roles or needs shift

Successful teams adjust their agreements as they learn more about how they work together.

## Explore your Health Problem

Your team was assigned one of the following topics.  For each topic, I have provided and example outcome or marker that might be used to measure this particular problem.  There may be other markers as well.

At this point in our process, take some time and do some preliminary research on your topic before you start the next module.

## List of Topics

Find your topic from the list below. Your topic was assigned by me to the team.
### Vaccine Uptake

Preventable illness and death persist because vaccine uptake emerges from interacting systems of trust, access, information, and policy rather than individual choice alone.  How can we stabilize and improve vaccine uptake? 

**Example outcome or marker:** Vaccination coverage rates stratified by geography, race or ethnicity, and insurance status.

### Opioid Epidemic

Opioid-related morbidity and mortality continue as prescribing practices, drug supply dynamics, economic conditions, and treatment access reinforce one another across health and social systems. How can we reduce death and injury from opioid overdose?  

**Example outcome or marker:** Opioid overdose death rates per 100,000 population, examined over time.

### Access to Mental Health Care

Mental health needs go unmet because financing structures, workforce availability, stigma, and care delivery models fail to align with population demand.  How could we improve access to mental health care?

**Example outcome or marker:** Percentage of adults with a diagnosed mental health condition who receive any mental health treatment in a given year.

### Access to Medical Care in Spanish-Speaking Communities

Spanish-speaking communities experience worse health outcomes due to language barriers, immigration-related fear, and institutional practices that limit meaningful access to care.  How can we improve access for Spanish-speaking communities.

**Example outcome or marker:** Percentage of Spanish-speaking adults with a usual source of care; Rate of avoidable emergency department use among Spanish-speaking patients.

### Access to Reproductive Care Including Abortion

Reproductive health outcomes are increasingly shaped by legal, geographic, and institutional constraints rather than clinical need or patient preference.  How can we increase access?

**Example outcome or marker:** Average travel distance or time required to obtain comprehensive reproductive health services including abortion care.

### Diabetes Management

Chronic diseases such as diabetes remain poorly controlled because health care, food systems, housing, employment, and insurance design interact over long time horizons.  How can we improve health outcomes?

**Example outcome or marker:** Proportion of adults with diabetes achieving glycemic control as measured by HbA1c levels.

### Health Impacts of Extreme Heat

Heat-related illness and death increase as housing quality, urban design, labor protections, and emergency response systems fail to adapt to rising temperatures.  

**Example outcome or marker:** Heat-related emergency department visits or mortality during extreme heat events.

### Emergency Department Overcrowding

Emergency department congestion persists as upstream access failures, payment incentives, staffing constraints, and patient flow dynamics reinforce reliance on acute care.  
**Example outcome or marker:** Average emergency department length of stay or inpatient boarding time.

### Maternal Morbidity and Mortality

Preventable maternal harm continues due to structural racism, fragmented prenatal care, insurance instability, and variation in hospital quality.  

**Example outcome or marker:** Maternal morbidity rates per 10,000 delivery hospitalizations.
### Medication Affordability and Adherence

Effective treatments fail to produce health gains because pricing, insurance design, and income volatility interact to undermine consistent medication use.  

**Example outcome or marker:** Proportion of patients reporting cost-related medication nonadherence.
### Housing Instability and Health

Poor health outcomes concentrate among people experiencing housing instability due to reinforcing feedback between housing markets, health care utilization, and social service systems.

**Example outcome or marker:** Rates of preventable hospitalizations or emergency department use among people experiencing housing instability.

The health problem provides **context and orientation**, not a single outcome that every system element must directly explain.
# Module Deliverables

## What to submit: 
- Your team agreement
## Format:  
For your team agreement, you must at a minimum answer these questions:

Answer every question. 

1. What platform will you use to communicate?
2. What are your expectations for response times?
3. How will you document your decisions?  Who will do it?
4. How frequent will you check-in together?
5. When and how will you meet?  
6. What is the meeting schedule?
7. How will you review each member's work together?
8. How will you make decisions on the team?
9. What are the responsibilities of each team member?
10. How will you set and enforce deadlines?
11. How will you redistribute tasks if necessary?
12. How will you manage conflict?
13. How will you handle it if someone falls behind?
14. What roles have you assigned? 
15. How will roles be shared?

By submitting your agreement on Canvas, you are indicating that every member of your team has been consulted and has explicitly agreed to to this agreement.

Include your class section, team number, and the names of your team members at the top of the page.

## Length:  
Your agreement should be approximately a page to two pages.

## Submission location: 
- Canvas
## How This Will Be Evaluated

This deliverable will be evaluated based on completeness. You are establishing how your team will function and coordinate work.
